description: Apollo 16 Flight Journal - Day 5, Part 1: Transfer to Lunar Module, Revs 10 and 11

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 91 hours, 24 minutes into the mission.
We're standing by now awaiting a wake-up call by CapCom Don Peterson to the crew of Apollo 16.
We show Apollo 16 presently with an apolune of 58.9 nautical miles [109.1 kilometres] and a perilune of 10.4 nautical miles [19.3 kilometres].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: How you down there this morning, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Got about three short items and a SIM bay status, if you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Can we hold off on the SIM bay status?
And I'll copy your three short items.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The first one is - based on our evaluation, your potassium levels are running a little low, and we'd like to recommend that you drink some orange juice this morning.
[Pause.] Also, you've got a long day ahead of you, so we'd like to recommend that you eat a bit more food.
[Pause.] Second item is terminate Battery Bravo charge.
The third item is terminate the jet-on monitor.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Number one, I understand your comment about the potassium low and all that jazz.
We've just finished up breakfast, and I think we've eaten almost everything that the LMies have to eat.
And we - we've been drinking all the drinks every day.
And, if we get a chance, well we'll try to get some more - get another juice bag out.

We will terminate the Battery B charge and terminate the Jet Monitor, and I understand the way to do the Jet Monitor is - I'll go to - I'll go to the SCS mode and key a Verb 37 Noun 20 and then verify that Noun 26 is all zeros again.
And I can go back to P20.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: 091:29:02 PetersonThere's no requirement to go to SCS, 16.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I can just call [Verb] 37 [Noun] 20 without getting any firings?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 91 hours, 30 minutes.
That's Ken Mattingly aboard Apollo 16 who has been speaking with CapCom Don Peterson here in Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 091:31:10 Peterson16, just to be sure we're talking the same thing - to kill the jet-on Monitor, we want to do a Verb 37 Enter, 30 Enter, a Verb 37 Enter, 20 Enter and zero the Noun 26.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Don, I can't get to the Updates Book where that's written down.
Would you read it to me slowly, and I'll do that terminate right now.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: 091:32:11 PetersonOkay, it's Verb 37 Enter, 30 Enter, Verb 37 Enter, 20 Enter, zero the Noun 26.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 091:32:31 PetersonAnd, 16, you're about one minute to LOS, and you're looking good.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we've - we're taking an extra orange with potassium this morning.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Take a look at that biomed off of me and see how it looks.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I got it reset - from yesterday.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: 091:33:28 PetersonKen, still looks a little loose.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 091:33:40 PetersonWe'll have to take a better look at it next time around, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is John, and the sensors are fixed.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: 091:34:01 PetersonWe've got confusion down here on who we're monitoring, John.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 091:34:42 PetersonJohn, the lead we're concerned with is the sternal lead - the one on your breastbone.
You might jiggle it a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It sure looks like it's all tight.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: 091:35:35 PetersonJohn, you might try putting new sponges on that lead.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: CM transcript starts at 091:35:43 and finishes 091:39:18.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'll pick you up next rev and talk about it.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 91 hours, 37 minutes into the mission.
We've had Loss of Signal with Apollo 16.
The last conversation aboard the spacecraft was John Young checking on his biomedical sensors.
We're at 91 hours, 37 minutes into the mission and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 92 hours, 15 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and some 13 minutes, 12 seconds until Apollo 16 reappears from behind the Moon in this tenth lunar orbit.
Gerry Griffin's Gold Team of flight controllers settled in for the day's activities leading up to powered descent and lunar landing.
The offgoing shift of flight controllers have practically all left the room having debriefed their replacements.
There will not be a change of shift briefing.
Repeat, will not be a change of shift briefing, in as much as the preceding shift was primarily a sleep shift.
Apollo 16 still in a 59 by 10.6-nautical-mile [109.3 by 19.6-kilometre] lunar orbit.
Current velocity - orbital velocity, 5,355 feet [1,632 metres] per second.
And current altitude at this point in the orbit, 44.8 nautical miles [83.0 kilometres].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 92 hours, 27 minutes into the mission of Apollo 16.
Less than a minute away from Apollo 16's reappearance around the eastern limb of the Moon on lunar orbit number 10.

On this next frontside pass, the main concern of the crew will be transferring quite a few of the pieces of gear and equipment into the Lunar Module, getting the Lunar Module prepared for the descent and landing at the Descartes landing site.
Six minutes away, we may be a few moments late getting acquisition because of the apparent position of the High Gain Antenna as seen on the ground by the communications officer.
Waiting for confirmation of Acquisition Of Signal by the tracking network.
Flight Director Gerry Griffing - Griffin is polling all of his flight controllers on the current status and preparation for the landing phase.
Getting all the details worked out.
Any minor problems the different positions - console positions might have.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're in the process of a little suit donning here, and we've got one problem.
When I went to retract the mass spectrometer, and the gamma ray boom came in fine, but the mass spectrometer boom indicates barber pole and has stayed barber pole about ten minutes.
I went ahead and enabled the jets.
And I'd like for you to take a look at the - at the - telemetry talkback on the boom, and see if you can suggest something for me to do.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We're wondering if you've gone through the malfunction procedure for that Boom Retraction on page 1-24 of the Systems Handbook?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Well, I looked and didn't find one that was appropriate.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We're pretty busy, as you knows Jim: and so I'd really appreciate it if you could talk me through whatever steps you want.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We'd like you to put the Mass Spec Boom switch to the Off position.
That's if it's not there already.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And when I do, it goes to gray.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We had understood that you had activated all your RCS jets.
We show you still in the SIM bay configuration.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: I have the switches with the circuit breakers I had left out.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: You can go normal acquisition procedures on the S-band.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Jim, what did you want me to do with that High Gain?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We wanted you to go through normal acquisition procedures.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: 16, will you go Auto on the High Gain?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We have SIM bay data now.
Will you go back to Retract on the - on the Boom switch?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Will you check the talkback on that boom, and tell us whether it's full barber pole or a partial?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: It's the same as it's been, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okays we copy; a full barber pole.
We show it partially retracted within acceptable limits.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: What do you mean partially retracted?
Does that mean your telemetry point has cinched closure?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, then I'll put it back into the Off position?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I think we - we want it in Retract.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Okay, I'll leave it in Retract.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, you could put the Retract switch back to the Off position.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: If you can watch that thing on telemetry, I can extend it enough to clear it and then try a Retract again.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, let's - We're within limits, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: On the docking latch number 10, I've got some observations, if you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, when I look in under the LM umbilical connector cover, the roller cam looks normal, and everything I can see from that side looks correct.

When I look under the cover on the right side - that's the probe connector cover - I look at the bungee in the - the little arm that sticks out from the bottom of the cam - when I compare it to another latch, it doesn't stick out as far; in fact, the back of this part - this inner piece of the bell crank - I can get the part number on it, and it's - it's flush; whereas, on the other end, it sticks out and it shows the pin; so it looks like it hasn't come over center.
Sorry I don't have the right part nomenclature.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: If you had a docking latch on the table there, somewhere, you could see what I'm looking at.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Can you see anything that might be obstructing or interfering with that cam action?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: if you go to P00 and Accept, we'll uplink you.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: You can go back to Block.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Houston, we just passed over the good landing site, and you can see the whole area.
Stands out very nicely just like the model.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Houston, from my present position I can't be sure exactly where I am because I just looked out the window again but - we passed over a large crater, and it has three little domes in the bottom of it with craters in the top of them; and they look like very subtle cinder cones.
I'll try to mark that guy on the way back.
It's one of a cluster of two large ones and several smaller ones.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Will you give us another reading on that docking tunnel index when you have an opportunity?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: It's still the same thing: minus 3.5.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: John and Charlie got their suits on and they're in the LM.
Is there any reason they shouldn't go ahead and power up and get some cooling?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Apollo 16 Commander John Young and Lunar Module Pilot Charlie Duke are running almost 40 minutes ahead of the Flight Plan, in as much as they've already donned their pressure suits and they're in the Lunar Module, preparing to power it up.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston; Orion on internal power at 93:07.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The reason for that undervoltage last night was that the - all the heaters just happened to come on at that particular time.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We were cycling over to relief valve at the time [chuckle].
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Did you get my call on the LM power at 93:07?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Some 25 minutes remaining now in this frontside pass in the tenth lunar orbit of Apollo 16 before the still docked Orion and Casper go around the corner on the western limb of the Moon.
At 93:09, still live on the tenth lunar orbit, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: S-Band Aux Sci switched to Off.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Houston, can I give you some torquing angles, or did you copy them?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, and we're in the maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The maneuver, referred to just then by Ken Mattingly aboard the Command Module Casper, was the maneuver to the undocking attitude which has a Roll 0, Pitch 104, and Yaw 0; just about on time as called for in the Flight Plan.
Some 14 minutes and 8 seconds until Loss of Signal, this is Apollo Control at 93:20.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Our plan on the mass spec boom is to leave it where it is, and we're going to ask John and Charlie to check it after undocking, with respect to position.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And when I was trying to talk to you before, I just wanted to give you the words on that undervoltage which y'all had last night.
The reason for that was the fact that all the heaters came on at the same time.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Okay, I guess maybe I jumped the gun, but it seemed to me like that - that Master Alarm came on instantly when I hit that switch, and it seemed like the only prudent thing to do was to undo what I just did.
[Pause.] I'm glad to hear that, though.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: And, Ken, if you're - got a few minutes there - I could give you a Flight Plan update, a very short one.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: I'm in the middle of - suit donning and canister change.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We have confirmation that the communications system aboard the Lunar Module is activated.
We're beginning to get data out of the LM.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Ken, will you have Orion select the aft antenna?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We're reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We'd have been a little bit further along, but we had trouble with John's zipper today.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Orion, will you go Down Voice Backup?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: If there's anyth - anything significant from Orion, will you relay, because there is excessive noise down here.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Five minutes away from Loss of Signal as Apollo 16 passes behind the Moon.
Lunar Module cabin now showing at 5.02 pounds per square inch pressure [34.6 kPa].
Suit pressure 4.98 [psi - 34.33 kPa] for both men.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We still got a lot of noise, but we don't have the big dish up yet.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Okay, we got a signal strength of about 3.9 on the Aft Omni.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You don't have your audio breaker in, do you?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Did you turn on your stuff?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I just put my mike closer to my mouth.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And talked up a little bit.
I've got my Master Volume up full.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, where are you at, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We've had Loss of Signal as Apollo 16, still docked at this time, passed behind the Moon during the end of the 10th lunar orbit.
The crew of Apollo 16 considerably ahead of the timeline and getting the Lunar Module manned and checked out.
All three crewmen suited at this time.
We'll come back up at acquisition at the start of lunar orbit revolution number 11, lunar orbit number 11, and at 93:35, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Short period of CM transcript from 093:54:31 to 094:01:38.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Short CM transcript portion from 094:21:59 until 094:22:15.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
